title: Gerard van Groesbeeck

infobox.value: 21 February 1578 by [Pope Gregory XIII](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_XIII "Pope Gregory XIII")
[Gerard van Groesbeeck's coat of arms](/wiki/File:Gerard_van_Groesbeek_(1517-1582).svg "Gerard van Groesbeeck's coat of arms")

infobox.key: Preceded by [Robert of Berghes](/wiki/Robert_of_Berghes "Robert of Berghes")
infobox.value: **[Prince-bishop of Liège](/wiki/Prince-bishop_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Prince-bishop of Liège")**  1565–1580 | Succeeded by [Ernest of Bavaria](/wiki/Ernest_of_Bavaria "Ernest of Bavaria")

(1517–1580) was a [prelate](/wiki/Prelate "Prelate") who became the 88th [Bishop of Liège](/wiki/Bishop_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Bishop of Liège"), as well as [Prince-Abbot](/wiki/Prince-abbot "Prince-abbot") of [Stavelot](/wiki/Principality_of_Stavelot-Malmedy "Principality of Stavelot-Malmedy") and a [cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholicism) "Cardinal (Catholicism)") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").

*Statuts et ordonnances touchant le stil et maniere de proceder & l'administration de justice devant, & par les courts & justices seculieres du païs de Liege* (Liège, [Gauthier Morberius](/wiki/Gauthier_Morberius "Gauthier Morberius"), 1572). [Online](https://donum.uliege.be/handle/2268.1/4260).
1565–1580 | Succeeded by [Ernest of Bavaria](/wiki/Ernest_of_Bavaria "Ernest of Bavaria") |

Belgian cardinal of the Catholic Church

**Gerard van Groesbeeck** (1517–1580) was a [prelate](/wiki/Prelate "Prelate") who became the 88th [Bishop of Liège](/wiki/Bishop_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Bishop of Liège"), as well as [Prince-Abbot](/wiki/Prince-abbot "Prince-abbot") of [Stavelot](/wiki/Principality_of_Stavelot-Malmedy "Principality of Stavelot-Malmedy") and a [cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholicism) "Cardinal (Catholicism)") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gerard_van_Groesbeeck&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Gerard van Groesbeeck was born at [Kuringen Castle](/w/index.php?title=Kuringen_Castle&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kuringen Castle (page does not exist)") outside [Hasselt](/wiki/Hasselt "Hasselt") in 1517.[[1]](#cite_note-bare_url-1) His parents were Jan, Baron of [Groesbeek](/wiki/Groesbeek "Groesbeek") (in [Guelders](/wiki/Guelders "Guelders")), and Berthe de Ghoër.[[2]](#cite_note-BNB-2) As a young man, he became a [canon](/wiki/Canon_(priest) "Canon (priest)") of [Aachen Cathedral](/wiki/Aachen_Cathedral "Aachen Cathedral"),[[1]](#cite_note-bare_url-1) and in 1548 the [dean](/wiki/Dean_(Christianity) "Dean (Christianity)") of the [cathedral chapter](/wiki/Cathedral_chapter "Cathedral chapter") of [St. Lambert's Cathedral, Liège](/wiki/St._Lambert%27s_Cathedral,_Li%C3%A8ge "St. Lambert's Cathedral, Liège").[[2]](#cite_note-BNB-2)

Groesbeeck was appointed coadjutor to [prince-bishop](/wiki/Prince-bishop "Prince-bishop") [Robert de Berghes](/wiki/Robert_de_Berghes "Robert de Berghes") in 1562, and was named his successor on 6 March 1563, while negotiations were in progress for Berghes' resignation. Groesbeeck became the [administrator of the diocese](/wiki/Administrator_of_the_diocese "Administrator of the diocese") on 11 April 1564.[[1]](#cite_note-bare_url-1)

## Prince-Bishop

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gerard_van_Groesbeeck&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prince-Bishop")]

On 5 June 1564 Groesbeeck was elected Prince-Bishop of Liège; his election was [preconized](/wiki/Preconization "Preconization") by [Pope Pius IV](/wiki/Pope_Pius_IV "Pope Pius IV") on 23 February 1565.[[1]](#cite_note-bare_url-1) He was [consecrated](/wiki/Consecration "Consecration") as a bishop by [Gregoire Silvius](/w/index.php?title=Gregoire_Silvius&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gregoire Silvius (page does not exist)"), [titular bishop](/wiki/Titular_bishop "Titular bishop") of [Tagaste](/wiki/Souk_Ahras "Souk Ahras"), in [Herkenrode Abbey](/wiki/Herkenrode_Abbey "Herkenrode Abbey") on 20 May 1565.[[1]](#cite_note-bare_url-1) He made his solemn entry into [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège") on 3 June and into [Maastricht](/wiki/Maastricht "Maastricht") on 17 June, before touring the "[bonnes villes](/wiki/Good_Cities "Good Cities")" of the principality to be inaugurated in each.[[2]](#cite_note-BNB-2)

In a session of the [Estates of the principality](/wiki/Estates_of_the_Realm "Estates of the Realm") in 1566, as Calvinists were beginning to preach openly in the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"), the bishop urged a more rigorous repression of heresy. He also demanded that the mayors of Liège entrust the keys to the city to his keeping, or in his absence to the dean of the cathedral, a demand that the city council opposed in a long-running on-and-off dispute that was resolved only in 1649.[[2]](#cite_note-BNB-2) He invited Jesuits to preach in the city of Liège, but not to establish a house there.[[2]](#cite_note-BNB-2)

In 1567, as the [Dutch Revolt](/wiki/Dutch_Revolt "Dutch Revolt") was beginning in the neighbouring [Habsburg Netherlands](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands"), the towns of [Hasselt](/wiki/Hasselt "Hasselt"), [Maaseik](/wiki/Maaseik "Maaseik"), [Stokkem](/wiki/Stokkem "Stokkem") and Maastricht openly rejected the bishop’s authority. Hasselt was brought to submit by military force. Maastricht, under the dual sovereignty of Liège and [Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant"), was obliged to accept a Spanish garrison by [Philip of Noircarmes](/wiki/Philip_of_Noircarmes "Philip of Noircarmes").[[2]](#cite_note-BNB-2) The citizens of Maaseik and Stokkem submitted to the prince-bishop rather than suffer a similar fate. With increasing numbers of Protestant refugees from the Habsburg Netherlands entering the principality, edicts were issued on 14 April 1567 and again on 27 September giving all foreigners three days' notice to depart from the territory, unless they were able to claim citizenship rights or could demonstrate that they had been living there peaceably for more than two years, with attestations that they had not participated in the [Iconoclastic Fury](/wiki/Iconoclastic_Fury "Iconoclastic Fury").[[2]](#cite_note-BNB-2)

While seeking to remain neutral in the [Eighty Years’ War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%E2%80%99_War "Eighty Years’ War"), Gerard van Groesbeeck was obliged to contend with infringements on his rights and territory by both the [Duke of Alva](/wiki/Duke_of_Alva "Duke of Alva") and the [Prince of Orange](/wiki/William_the_Silent "William the Silent"), particularly affecting [Tongeren](/wiki/Tongeren "Tongeren"), [Sint-Truiden](/wiki/Sint-Truiden "Sint-Truiden"), [Saint-Hubert](/wiki/Saint-Hubert,_Belgium "Saint-Hubert, Belgium") and [Hastière](/wiki/Hasti%C3%A8re "Hastière"), all of which were plundered by one side or the other.[[2]](#cite_note-BNB-2) On 28 October 1568 Orange appeared before the walls of Liège with an army, demanding passage and promising to respect the city’s liberties, but was refused entry. He assaulted the city and began siege works, but quickly abandoned them, burning the convents of Saint-Laurent, Saint-Gilles and Val-Benoît before retreating.[[2]](#cite_note-BNB-2) He and the Estates of Liège declared the Prince-Bishopric a [neutral state](/wiki/Neutrality_(international_relations) "Neutrality (international relations)") in 1577.[[1]](#cite_note-bare_url-1)

Under Gerard van Groesbeeck's authority, a new codification of the legal procedures in the principality's courts was promulgated on 3 July 1572.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In 1574, a letter from [Pope Gregory XIII](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_XIII "Pope Gregory XIII") congratulating Groesbeeck on his conduct was published.[[2]](#cite_note-BNB-2) In 1576, he became Prince-Abbot of Stavelot.[[1]](#cite_note-bare_url-1) Gregory made him a [cardinal priest](/wiki/Cardinal_priest "Cardinal priest") in the [consistory](/wiki/Papal_consistory "Papal consistory") of 21 February 1578,[[1]](#cite_note-bare_url-1) but he never traveled to [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") to receive the [red hat](/wiki/Galero "Galero") or a [titular church](/wiki/Titular_church "Titular church").[[1]](#cite_note-bare_url-1)

In 1577, when [Margaret of Valois](/wiki/Margaret_of_Valois "Margaret of Valois") was travelling to [Spa](/wiki/Spa,_Belgium "Spa, Belgium"), Groesbeeck lent her his palace, himself temporarily moving to the [Abbey of Saint-Jacques](/wiki/St_James%27s_Church,_Li%C3%A8ge "St James's Church, Liège"). The royal visit was the occasion for a series of celebrations, banquets and exchanges of gifts. She described her host as "a lord accompanied by much virtue, prudence and goodness, who speaks French well; agreeable of person, honourable, magnificent and agreeable company."[[2]](#cite_note-BNB-2)

He died on 29 December 1580 and was buried in St. Lambert's Cathedral, Liège.[[2]](#cite_note-BNB-2)
